content:
2 min read
Just now

--

When I started programming, one of the first platforms I used was HackerRank. After that, I moved on to LeetCode, and I also spent some time on GeeksforGeeks.
Each of these platforms helped me in different ways, but one thing I noticed across all of them is that they mostly focus on algorithms and structured problem solving.
Don’t get me wrong, that is very important because it builds strong fundamentals.
But over time I started thinking about something deeper. How do we prepare people to solve real business problems, not just algorithmic puzzles?
That question is what led me to appreciate the idea behind Data Skills Academy.
In real data roles, you are rarely just solving abstract problems. Most of the time you are trying to understand what is happening in a business. You ask questions like why numbers are changing, how different groups behave, and how to turn raw data into meaningful insight.
That is why I found the idea of combining both approaches interesting. Algorithmic thinking from platforms like LeetCode and HackerRank, together with business focused data problems that reflect real scenarios.
So instead of only asking if someone can solve a technical puzzle, we also ask if they can solve a business problem using data.
Here is a short breakdown of each platform and what makes them unique.
HackerRank is often a starting point for many learners. It focuses on structured problems that help you learn syntax, basic algorithms, and interview preparation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Data Skills Academy focuses more on real world data scenarios. It emphasizes business style questions where the goal is not just to compute, but to interpret and understand data.
LeetCode goes deeper into algorithmic thinking. It focuses on patterns, optimization, and preparing for technical interviews in top tech companies.
GeeksforGeeks is more educational and theory driven. It explains concepts clearly and provides many classic interview problems with detailed explanations.
At the end of the day, all these platforms are valuable, but they each train a different way of thinking.
The real gap, in my opinion, is bridging algorithmic problem solving with real business data thinking.
